query: correlation between humeral and glenoidal lesions in chronic anterior post-traumatic shoulder dislocations

retrieved_docs.title: Anterior Shoulder Instability -- Pathophysiology -- Glenoid Sided Defects
retrieved_docs.content: The nature of glenoid defects is best appreciated on three-dimensional CT scans, having the ability to differentiate between a true bony Bankart lesion from the more erosive changes that can be seen representing an attritional bone loss state. The former is considered a true fracture fragment containing a portion of the anterior glenoid rim. The latter often appears rounded off with blunted edges on imaging, indicative of a more chronic, erosive process secondary to recurrent instability or, in the acute setting, a low-energy compression fracture. The description of the location of these defects often references relative to a clock face created by drawing a circle around the glenoid. Saito et al. reported that the most common location of glenoid defects in the setting of anterior instability is between 2:30 and 4:20. [32] The pathologic mechanism of glenoid bone defects is likely multifactorial. In general, blunted osseous glenoid defects can present in the setting of either:

retrieved_docs.title: Anterior Shoulder Instability -- Pathophysiology -- Glenoid Sided Defects
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic attritional glenoid erosive changes can develop over time following a high-energy index dislocation event that initially suffers an accompanying glenoid injury. The injury is either managed nonoperatively or ultimately fails surgical fixation to result in subsequent instability events with progressive attritional glenoid erosion over time.

retrieved_docs.title: Anterior Glenohumeral Joint Dislocation -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Recurrence and prognosis are highly dependent on age and activity level. Multiple studies cite incidence rates with certain contact sports. There is an up to 92% recurrent shoulder dislocation rate in patients less than 25 years old and participating in a contact sport. In patients over 30, the recurrence is approximately 72%. Depending on the initial age of injury, follow up, and Bankart lesion repair, patients may develop arthropathy in addition to chronic instability. Arthropathy is seen in approximately 28% to 67% of those that undergo a Bankart repair, which may be due to chondral damage due to repeated dislocations. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Shoulder Arthrogram -- Clinical Significance -- Glenohumeral Instability and Labroligamentous Abnormal Findings
retrieved_docs.content: Osseous assessment and the glenoid track concept: The recognition that recurrent instability is often driven by bone loss has revolutionized the management of this condition. While large osseous defects can be visualized on MRA, CT and CTA modalities are considered the gold standard due to their precise quantification capabilities. [94] This assessment is mandatory in cases of recurrent instability or when considering surgery. Hill-Sachs lesion: A compression fracture of the posterosuperior humeral head, caused by impaction against the anterior glenoid rim during dislocation. [95] Glenoid bone loss: A fracture or chronic erosion of the anteroinferior glenoid rim (a "bony Bankart" is the acute fracture fragment). The circle method, applied to CT images, is the standard approach for quantifying bone loss, calculating  the percentage of the glenoid that has been lost relative to its total width. [96] The glenoid track concept: This critical concept mathematically relates the size and location of the Hill-Sachs lesion to the amount of glenoid bone loss. [97] The "glenoid track" is the zone of contact between the glenoid and the humeral head during normal motion. If the Hill-Sachs lesion is larger than the glenoid track (an "off-track" lesion), the lesion can engage with the edge of the glenoid during abduction and external rotation, leading to a high risk of redislocation. [94] Imaging, particularly 3D-CT, is used to measure the glenoid track and determine if a lesion is "on-track" or "off-track." This determination directly guides surgical decision-making. "On-track" lesions may be treated with a standard arthroscopic Bankart repair, whereas "off-track" lesions often require a bone-augmenting procedure, eg, the Latarjet procedure, to restore stability. [97]

retrieved_docs.content: Acute, low-energy, compression fracture Chronic/recurrent, low-energy, attritional deficiency and erosive changes over time

retrieved_docs.title: Shoulder Arthrogram -- Clinical Significance -- Glenohumeral Instability and Labroligamentous Abnormal Findings
retrieved_docs.content: Soft-tissue lesions: MRA is the gold standard for delineating the spectrum of injuries to the capsulolabral complex that result from dislocation events, as these lesions are best seen on MRA. [89] Bankart lesion : The classic injury, defined as an avulsion of the anteroinferior labrum and the attached inferior glenohumeral ligament (IGHL) from the glenoid rim. On MRA, contrast is seen extending between the detached labrum and the glenoid. Bankart variants Perthes lesion: A nondisplaced Bankart lesion where the labrum is avulsed with an intact but stripped scapular periosteum. Contrast can be seen beneath the labrum, which remains in a near-anatomic position. [90] ALPSA (anterior labral ligamentous periosteal sleeve avulsion) lesion: The avulsed anteroinferior labroligamentous complex displaces medially and rotates inferiorly, scarring down to the glenoid neck deep to an intact periosteum. This can be a cause of failed Bankart repair if not recognized. [91] GLAD (glenolabral articular disruption) lesion: A superficial tear of the anterior labrum associated with an adjacent chondral injury of the glenoid articular cartilage. The labrum is not typically displaced. [92]

retrieved_docs.title: Anteroinferior Glenoid Labrum Lesion (Bankart Lesion) -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Lesions of the glenoid labrum are most commonly the result of traumatic shoulder dislocation. [7] The most common cause of traumatic shoulder dislocation is participation in collision or contact sports. [8] Primary or recurrent shoulder dislocations result in varying degrees of injury to the glenoid labrum, such as Bankart lesion, SLAP (Superior Labral lesion Anterior to Posterior) lesion, ALPSA (Anterior Labral Periosteal Sleeve Avulsion) lesion, and HAGL (Humeral Avulsion of Glenohumeral Ligament) lesion. Bankart lesion is specifically caused by anterior shoulder dislocation with the shoulder in external rotation and abduction. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Anteroinferior Glenoid Labrum Lesion (Bankart Lesion) -- History and Physical -- History
retrieved_docs.content: Evaluation of the Bankart’s lesion requires a thorough history, including the index dislocation, number of dislocations or subluxations, and their reduction method (self or medical personnel). Clinicians must enquire about the patient’s occupation, involvement in the contact sport, mechanism of trauma, and history of the epileptic disorder. [25] The history of a dislocation includes the following key points – Demographic details Occupation Level of activity- contact sport, recreational, none Index dislocation Age at first dislocation Number of dislocations/subluxations Mechanism of dislocation Presence of associated injury Reduction of dislocation- self or medical person Dislocation in sleep History of epileptic disorder

retrieved_docs.title: Anteroinferior Glenoid Labrum Lesion (Bankart Lesion) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The glenoid labrum is a fibrocartilaginous ring attached circumferentially to the glenoid rim contributing to the stability of the shoulder joint. [1] The glenoid labrum increases the glenoid surface vertically by 75% and horizontally by 57%, according to Saha et al. [2] Bankart's lesion represents an anterior and inferior labral detachment from the glenoid with an associated capsuloligamentous injury below the equator of the glenoid. [3] This result in anterior instability of the shoulder joint due to the loss of the following normal mechanisms, such as the choke block effect of the labrum, concavity compression mechanism of the rotator cuff, and function of the inferior glenohumeral ligament. [4] Traumatic subluxations or dislocations of the shoulder joint cause injury to the glenoid labrum. [5] Almost 87 to 100% of the index anterior shoulder dislocations are associated with Bankart lesions. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Anterior Shoulder Instability -- Pathophysiology -- Long-term Implications
retrieved_docs.content: Glenoid bone loss decreases the available articular arc and generates glenoid-humeral mismatch. The former generates a smaller articular arc length and decreased surface area by which the glenoid cavity can resist transmitted axial forces from the proximal humerus. Thus, failing to address glenoid bone loss during surgical fixation will result in significant shear forces imparted to a repaired capsulolabral interface. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Glenolabral Articular Disruption (GLAD) -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The vague presenting features of GLAD lesions, and their association with both unstable and stable shoulder injuries, means that a broad differential diagnosis should be considered. This should include any traumatic glenolabral pathology causing either anterior or global shoulder discomfort. Such lesions may consist of: Common traumatic labral tears, tearing of the labrum and associated ligaments partially or completely off the glenoid, most commonly the anterior-inferior labrum (Bankart lesions) [17] Anterior-Inferior instability lesions that include a glenoid rim fracture - bony Bankart lesions [17] Perthes lesion – a labral complex injury, but the labrum is still attached to the glenoid via a periosteal sleeve [18] Anterior Ligamentous Periosteal Sleeve Avulsion – another labral injury, but it displaces medially on the glenoid neck [18] (HAGL) or Bony HAGL – this time, the anterior-inferior glenohumeral ligament is avulsed from the humeral rather than labral attachment [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Superior Labrum Anterior Posterior Lesions -- Etiology -- Peel-back Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Compared to the acute, traumatic SLAP injuries, the overhead athlete is more likely to present with attritional-based etiologies. In these scenarios, SLAP tears present with the insidious onset and progressive deep shoulder pain in young athletes with the arm in the abduction and external rotation position during the late-cocking phase of throwing.  In this position, the force on the biceps coupled with the posterior glide of the humerus results in the peeling off of the posterosuperior quadrant of the glenoid and posterior labrum. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Anterior Shoulder Instability -- Evaluation -- Pertinent Findings
retrieved_docs.content: The axillary lateral view provides essential information about the position of the humeral head relative to the glenoid. Additionally, the presence and degree of anterior (or posterior) glenoid erosion or glenoid dysplasia, as well as any potential humeral head defects, can be appreciated with this view as well. Anterior glenoid rim fractures or glenoid avulsion injuries may also be present. The presence of medial neck scalloping or a possible avulsion injury is visible on AP imaging in the presence of an associated humeral avulsion of glenohumeral ligaments (HAGL). [54]

retrieved_docs.title: Anteroinferior Glenoid Labrum Lesion (Bankart Lesion) -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: An anterior shoulder dislocation is associated with a Bankart lesion in 87 to 100%, a Hill-Sachs lesion in 90%, a bony Bankart lesion in 73%, a rotator cuff injury in 13%, and a SLAP (Superior Labral lesion Anterior to Posterior) in 10%. [14] [15] [16] Perthes lesion, Anterior Labral Periosteal Sleeve Avulsion (ALPSA), Gleno-Labral Articular Disruption (GLAD), and Humeral avulsion of Glenohumeral Ligament (HAGL) occur in a minority of patients. [16] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Superior Labrum Anterior Posterior Lesions -- History and Physical -- Shoulder Exam
retrieved_docs.content: A detailed sensory examination should take place in all acute and chronic instability patients.  In addition to axillary nerve function, motor function of the elbow, wrist, and hand should undergo an assessment to rule out the possibility of a brachial plexus injury associated with the dislocation. Distal pulses should be assessed at the wrist as well.

retrieved_docs.title: Anterior Shoulder Instability -- Pathophysiology -- Long-term Implications
retrieved_docs.content: By definition, dislocation arthropathy describes degenerative changes of the shoulder joint. Hovelius and Rahme conducted a prospective study of 257 primary anterior shoulder dislocations in 255 patients. At a 25-year follow-up, 27% had mild glenohumeral arthritis, and 34% had moderate to severe arthrosis. [39] Although the role of recurrent dislocations is unclear, the pathophysiology underlying the development of dislocation arthropathy may be related to acute changes during the primary or recurrent traumatic instability events, the ensuing altered glenohumeral joint biomechanics, and/or attritional changes that occur secondary to the recurrent instability.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2392
retrieved_docs.content: The choroidea (choroid) serves as the anatomic location for a wide spectrum of ocular pathologies. Disseminated choroiditis, disseminated scar of the retina and choroid, focal choroiditis, generalized disseminated choroiditis and chorioretinitis, juxtapapillary focal choroiditis and chorioretinitis, partial generalized choroidal dystrophy, peripheral focal retinitis and retinochoroiditis, post‑inflammatory chorioretinal scar, post‑inflammatory choroid scar, postsurgical choroid scar, and post‑traumatic chorioretinal scar are all conditions in which the choroidea is identified as the finding site. In addition, the choroidea is linked to specific lesion descriptors such as “التمزق المشيمي” (choroidal tear), “التهاب الشبكية والمشيمية المنتشر، غير محدد” (unspecified disseminated chorioretinitis), “التهاب المشيمية” (choroiditis), “التهاب المشيمية والشبكية البؤري، غير محدد” (unspecified focal choroiditis and retinitis), “انتشار الضمور الثانوي بالمشيمية” (secondary choroidal atrophy), “انفصال المشيمية المصلية” and “انفصال مشيمية العين” (serous choroidal detachment), and “ندبة مشيمية شبكية” (retinal‑choroidal scar). Collectively, these relationships underscore the choroidea’s central role as the site of inflammatory, degenerative, traumatic, and postoperative ocular findings, highlighting its importance in diagnostic evaluation and management of a variety of chorioretinal disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 1223
retrieved_docs.content: The choroidea (choroid) is the primary anatomic site for a broad spectrum of ocular inflammatory, traumatic, degenerative, neoplastic and metastatic conditions. Localized inflammatory entities include disseminated choroiditis, focal choroiditis, isolated choroiditis (التهاب المشيمية), partial generalized choroidal dystrophy, generalized disseminated choroiditis with chorioretinitis, peripheral disseminated choroiditis and chorioretinitis, juxtapapillary focal choroiditis with chorioretinitis, and unspecified widespread retino‑choroiditis (التهاب الشبكية والمشيمية المنتشر، غير محدد). Combined retinal‑choroidal lesions are represented by disseminated scar of retina AND choroid and by retinal choroidal scar (ندبة مشيمية شبكية). Traumatic sequelae include post‑traumatic chorioretinal scar and post‑traumatic choroid scar. Metastatic spread may involve the choroidea, exemplified by metastatic disseminated retinitis, metastatic disseminated retinitis AND retinochoroiditis, and metastatic disseminated retinitis AND retinochoroiditis with choroidal involvement. Vascular and developmental abnormalities comprise choroidal hemorrhage (نزيف مشيمي), brisk choroidal hemorrhage (نزيف مشيمي باثق), familial choroidal malformation (الأَتْلامُ وِعائِيَّةُ الشَّكل بالمشيمية), serous choroidal detachment (انفصال المشيمية المصلية), choroidal calcific degeneration (تنكس حصوي), general choroidal atrophy (تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة), extensive peripapillary choroidal calcification (حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي) and hereditary choroidal calcification (حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي). Collectively, these relationships underscore the choroidea’s central role as the locus of diverse pathological processes, highlighting the necessity of thorough choroidal assessment for accurate diagnosis, monitoring and management of a wide array of ocular diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 818
retrieved_docs.content: Various ocular conditions are documented as having the choroidea (choroid) as their primary finding site. Inflammatory entities such as disseminated choroiditis and chorioretinitis of the posterior pole, generalized disseminated choroiditis and chorioretinitis, focal choroiditis, juxt papillary focal choroiditis and chorioretinitis, peripheral focal retinitis and retinochoroiditis, as well as the Arabic‑language descriptions “التهاب الشبكية والمشيمية المنتشر، غير محدد” (diffuse retinal and choroidal inflammation, unspecified), “التهاب المشيمية والشبكية” (choroidal and retinal inflammation), and “التهاب المشيمية والشبكية البؤري، غير محدد” (focal choroidal and retinal inflammation, unspecified), all localize to the choroid. Traumatic and postoperative sequelae also involve the choroid: post‑traumatic chorioretinal scar, post‑traumatic choroid scar, postsurgical choroid scar, and the Dutch‑language finding “litteken; choroïdoretinaal, postoperatief” (post‑operative choroid‑retinal scar). Additional scar‑related findings are captured by “litteken; choroidea” (choroidal scar) and “litteken; choroïdoretinaal, postoperatief”. Structural disruptions of the choroid are represented by “التمزق المشيمي” (choroidal tear), serous detachments such as “انفصال المشيمية المصلية” (serous choroidal detachment) and “انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي” (hemorrhagic choroidal detachment). Degenerative and calcific changes are noted with “تنكس حصوي” (calcific degeneration), “تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة” (choroidal degeneration), and “حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي” (calcific choroidal plaque around the optic disc). Collectively, these entities illustrate that the choroid serves as a common anatomical substrate for a wide spectrum of inflammatory, traumatic, postoperative, detachment, and degenerative ocular pathologies, underscoring its clinical relevance in diagnosis and management of eye disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 698
retrieved_docs.content: The choroidea (choroid) is repeatedly identified as the primary anatomic site for a broad spectrum of ocular pathologies. Inflammatory disorders localized to the choroid include disseminated choroiditis, focal choroiditis, generalized disseminated choroiditis with chorioretinitis, peripheral disseminated choroiditis with chorioretinitis, metastatic disseminated retinitis‑retinochoroiditis, as well as combined entities such as التهاب المشيمية (choroiditis), التهاب المشيمية والشبكية (choroiditis‑retinitis), and the unspecified focal variant التهاب المشيمية والشبكية البؤري، غير محدد. Post‑inflammatory and post‑traumatic sequelae—post‑inflammatory chorioretinal scar, post‑inflammatory choroid scar, and post‑traumatic chorioretinal scar—are also situated in the choroidal tissue. Degenerative and structural changes affecting the choroidea comprise تنكس حصوي (calcific degeneration), تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة (choroidal degeneration), and choroidal sclerosis around the optic disc in both partial (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي) and total (حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي) forms. Vascular complications recorded as choroidal findings include نزيف مشيمي باثق (persistent choroidal hemorrhage), hemorrhagic choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي), and non‑hemorrhagic choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين). The choroidea is additionally noted to have a relationship with بُقَعُ روث, although the clinical significance of this association is not detailed. Moreover, the choroidea is described in Arabic as “حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي,” indicating that pathological observations may be localized to a specific genetic chorionic region. Recognizing the choroid as the common locus for these inflammatory, infectious, traumatic, degenerative, vascular, and post‑injury scarring processes—and for its genetic and ancillary associations—is essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted imaging, and appropriate therapeutic planning in ophthalmic practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2475
retrieved_docs.content: Various ocular disorders are identified as having the choroidea (choroid) as their primary finding site. Inflammatory conditions such as disseminated choroiditis, generalized disseminated choroiditis and chorioretinitis, and the unspecified “التهاب الشبكية والمشيمية المنتشر، غير محدد” (widespread retinal and choroidal inflammation) all involve the choroid. Specific inflammatory entities include acute posterior multifocal placoid pigment epitheliopathy (APMPPE) and “التهاب المشيمية والشبكية” (choroiditis and retinitis). Traumatic and postoperative sequelae are also choroidal, exemplified by postsurgical choroid scar and post‑traumatic chorioretinal scar. Degenerative processes affecting the choroid are represented by partial generalized choroidal dystrophy, “الضمور الشيخوخي بالمشيمية” (age‑related choroidal atrophy), “انتشار الضمور الثانوي بالمشيمية” (secondary choroidal atrophy spread), and “تنكس حصوي” as well as “تَنَكُّسُ المَشِّية” (calcific and general choroidal degeneration). Vascular‑related and structural abnormalities include choroidal detachment (“انفصال مشيمية العين”), peripapillary choroidal calcification (“حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي”), and familial retinal pigment epithelium disorders (“الأَتْلامُ وِعائِيَّةُ الشَّكل بالمشيمية”). Additional focal lesions such as juxtapapillary focal choroiditis and chorioretinitis, peripheral focal retinitis and retinochoroiditis, and disseminated scar of retina and choroid further illustrate the choroid’s role as a common site for both inflammatory and scar‑forming processes. Across these entities, the choroidea consistently serves as the anatomical location where pathological findings—ranging from scars (“litteken; choroidea”) to dystrophies and inflammatory plaques—are observed, underscoring its central importance in ocular disease assessment and management.

Degenerative changes are represented by partial generalized choroidal dystrophy, senile choroidal atrophy (الضمور الشيخوخي بالمشيمية), and secondary spread of choroidal atrophy (انتشار الضمور الثانوي بالمشيمية), reflecting age‑related or progressive loss of choroidal structure and function. Traumatic disruptions of the choroid include choroidal rupture (التمزق المشيمي) and choroidal inversion (تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة), while fluid‑related detachments are described as serous choroidal detachment (انفصال المشيمية المصلية) and general choroidal detachment of the eye (انفصال مشيمية العين). Congenital and hereditary abnormalities are captured by the terms congenital malformation of the choroid (الأَتْلامُ وِعائِيَّةُ الشَّكل بالمشيمية), partial perimacular choroidal calcification (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي), and hereditary choroidal calcification (حَثَلُ

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 1080
retrieved_docs.content: The choroidea (choroid) is a principal anatomic site for a broad spectrum of ocular diseases, serving as the locus for hemorrhagic events (نزيف مشيمي – choroidal hemorrhage), tears (التمزق المشيمي – choroidal tear), atrophic changes (الضمور الشيخوخي بالمشيمية – senile choroidal atrophy; انتشار الضمور الثانوي بالمشيمية – secondary choroidal atrophy spread; تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة – choroidal atrophy; تنكس حصوي – calcific degeneration), serous and hemorrhagic detachments (انفصال المشيمية المصلية – serous choroidal detachment; انفصال مشيمية العين – choroidal detachment of the eye; انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي – hemorrhagic choroidal detachment), and sclerotic lesions (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي – partial choroidal sclerosis surrounding the macula; حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي – total choroidal sclerosis surrounding the macula). A wide array of inflammatory and infectious processes also involve the choroidea, including Disseminated choroiditis, chorioretinitis of the posterior pole, Focal choroiditis, Generalized disseminated choroiditis AND chorioretinitis, Juxtapapillary focal choroiditis AND chorioretinitis, Peripheral focal retinitis AND retinochoroiditis, and unspecified widespread retino‑choroiditis (الالتهاب الشبكية والمشيمية المنتشر، غير محدد). Post‑inflammatory and traumatic sequelae frequently produce scarring (Postinflammatory chorioretinal scar, Postinflammatory choroid scar, Postsurgical choroid scar, Posttraumatic chorioretinal scar, Posttraumatic choroid scar, ندبة مشيمية شبكية – chorioretinal scar). The choroidea is also linked to بُقَعُ روث. Collectively, these entities underscore the choroid’s central role in ocular disease assessment, diagnosis, and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 1254
retrieved_docs.content: choroidal hemorrhage (حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي) and chorioretinal scar (ندبة مشيمية شبكية) are also identified at the choroidal level, the former suggesting underlying genetic vascular disorders and the latter reflecting prior injury or disease at the retinal‑choroidal interface. Collectively, these entities demonstrate that the choroidea serves as the common site for diverse inflammatory, scar‑forming, traumatic, postoperative, degenerative, hemorrhagic and hereditary eye conditions, underscoring its central role in the diagnosis, monitoring and management of a broad range of ocular diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 611
retrieved_docs.content: The choroidea (choroid) is the anatomical substrate for a broad spectrum of ocular pathologies, including acute vascular events such as choroidal hemorrhage (نزيف مشيمي) and choroidal tear (التمزق المشيمي). It is the primary site of numerous inflammatory disorders: disseminated choroiditis, focal choroiditis, partial generalized choroidal dystrophy, peripheral disseminated choroiditis, chorioretinitis, peripheral focal retinitis, retinochoroiditis, unspecified disseminated retinochoroiditis (التهاب الشبكية والمشيمية المنتشر، غير محدد), choroiditis (التهاب المشيمية), combined choroiditis and retinitis (التهاب المشيمية والشبكية), and acute posterior multifocal placoid pigment epitheliopathy (APMPPE, اعتلال الظهارة الصباغي اللوحي متعدد البؤر الخلفي الحاد). These inflammatory processes may involve the posterior pole or be widespread. Post‑injury and post‑procedure sequelae such as postinflammatory chorioretinal scar, postsurgical choroid scar, posttraumatic choroid scar, and retinal choroidal scar (ندبة مشيمية شبكية) demonstrate healing‑related lesions within the choroid. Degenerative and calcific changes include secondary choroidal atrophy spread (انتشار الضمور الثانوي بالمشيمية), choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين), sclerotic degeneration (تنكس حصوي), generalized choroidal calcification around the globe (حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي), and hereditary choroidal calcification (حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي). Collectively, these entities underscore the choroidea’s central role in hemorrhagic, traumatic, inflammatory, postoperative, and degenerative eye diseases, guiding clinicians in diagnosis, monitoring, and targeted management.
